## [00:00 - 03:08]

Hi, this is Don Crowther with a video about the Replay Report Strategy. So this is an optional strategy where you offer a complete PDF report on the subject of your webinar after you've run the webinar. Basically this report is a transcript of your webinar. What I like to do is I like to give them a transcript and actually add in slides so that they get a visual breakdown. And basically I'm trying to give them sort of the entire experience of the webinar for that group of people that didn't watch the webinar. So I'm giving them what I call a super transcript where it's transcript, but there's other stuff in it that makes it much easier to consume and the attempt is to replicate the experience of the webinar. But the last section of the webinar where I'm making the offer is not a transcript. It's more of a long form sales page in print format and it actually ends with a buy button. Yes, a buy button in a report. I know that most people don't think of that, but you know what, you can do it. It also references the recording within that transcript so that they can watch it too. Now here's how I create these. Immediately after the webinar, I get it transcribed. You can do that using rev.com, which is done by a human. This is a dollar a minute or something like temi.com, which is done by artificial intelligence. It's 10 cents per minute, but it's not quite as good. So and there's nothing says that these are the only two. There's hundreds of places you can get you can get transcripts done out there. Then I have someone edit them and add in the slides and add in the sales offer section, which I have prepared separately. Now I actually don't do this part myself because I am just terribly embarrassed whenever I go through transcripts of myself doing a webinar. I speak in one giant run on sentence and it's always like emotionally tough for me to go through and do this editing myself. And so I just have somebody else do it, but I haven't do it very quickly. So the ideal thing is to have this ready the next day. Bonus tip. I like to clearly indicate in that transcript the cart close date or the date that my special offer disappears. Another bonus tip. I like to use a tracking URL. And if you Google the term UTM variable builder on my buy button in that special report so

## [03:08 - 06:09]

that I can attribute any cells that come from the report to that report. And so again, you Google this term UTM variable builder. There's several of them that comes that come up and you're simply replacing the URL that that button goes to with one that's got some other things on the end of it that make it so that you can easily see exactly which cells came from that in your Google Analytics account. I find this strategy works, but I only use it when I'm working with a large audience because I'm going to spend extra time and effort and money doing this. And so I want to make sure that that is spent. It's a worthwhile spend. I also, by the way, find that this strategy actually generates results for months and years. And so what I do is once the cart close date occurs, I have the that page that the buy button originally sent them to redirect to another page once that card is closed that might say, hey, that offer is expired. But if you get it now, you can still get these bonuses or this special price or whatever so that people who are coming through that particular offer, they still get an expired price, but they have a deal versus what it would normally be on the website at the time. Or if you literally close cart, I would have a backdoor page for that so they can still get in the minute, but you'd be able to get in at the same price or the same offer, but they can still get in. And when you do this, I like to replace email three in the email in the replay promo sequence with an email talking about this. So that's where I work it into the sequence. And I also talk about this report in email number four. Also, so when do you use this strategy? I like to use this strategy when I've got a large audience going through the webinar funnel in this case. So lots of people are registered, or I'm going to run the webinar multiple times, or I'm going to turn this webinar into an evergreen webinar. So anytime I've got any of those three factors, I strongly consider doing this particular strategy. I also use it when I've got a content section that teaches something step by step, where they can actually get value out of a printable report that shows them here's step one, here's step two, etc. going down, because that's a usage occasion that the printed report does an amazing job of delivering on. Very frankly, I also do this when I'm working with a less sophisticated audience. So if I'm working with an audience who may not get the whole webinar thing and buying

## [06:09 - 06:46]

online, they may sometimes if I'm working with an audience that likes to print things out and highlight them and take notes in the margin and those kinds of things. That's a good audience for doing this. And also another place I do this is my webinar doesn't convert as well as I wanted it to oftentimes throw this in immediately. So if at the end of the webinar, look at those numbers and it didn't convert as well as I wanted it to, I'll immediately go and get it transcribed so that I have something else to be able to send out to people to give them additional value.
